Recovery Services vaults are concentrated in eastus (68.9%) and overwhelmingly use the RS0 SKU (97.8%) with infrastructure encryption enabled (100%): this shows an existing organizational preference for RS0 vaults in eastus with strong encryption, so deploy Azure Site Recovery (ASR) vaults in eastus using RS0, enable infrastructure encryption, and align vault placement with the tenant convention to simplify operations and compliance.

Automation accounts are present (3) with runbooks located in eastus and westus2 and Automation accounts set to public network access (100%) and Basic SKU: because runbook execution is already regionalized to these locations and uses Basic automation accounts, provision Automation accounts and runbooks in the same region(s), use managed identities for runbook authentication, and connect Automation to the target Log Analytics workspace to preserve job history for failover procedures.

User-assigned managed identities are widely used (370 UAI) and nearly all VMs expose both system and user-assigned identities (VMs: 98.8% have SystemAssigned, UserAssigned): this indicates a tenant preference for re-usable UAI-based service authentication, so attach a user-assigned managed identity to ASR components and to Automation runbooks for least-privilege RBAC access and to simplify secretless access during failover.

Key Vaults enforce strong retention and protection (soft delete enabled 100% with 90-day retention for 94.4% and purge protection enabled 100%): because the tenant already protects secrets with long retention and purge protection, store runbook credentials, ASR encryption keys and any recovery certificates in Key Vault with purge protection and the same retention policy to meet audit and compliance requirements.

Operational Insights (Log Analytics) is widely deployed (149 workspaces) with PerGB2018 SKU (87.9%) and public ingestion enabled (≈98%): since telemetry centralization is the norm, configure ASR vault and Automation diagnostic settings to forward replication and runbook logs to the tenant Log Analytics workspace(s) and create saved queries/alerts to produce an auditable failover timeline.

Storage accounts show mixed redundancy (Standard_LRS 48.5%, Standard_GRS 23.3%, Standard_RAGRS 17.2%): this demonstrates existing variance in redundancy choices, so for ASR target storage select geo-redundant options (GRS/RA-GRS) where regulatory RTO/RPO require cross-region durability or document why LRS is acceptable, and ensure storage redundancy settings match compliance objectives.

Network building blocks are pervasive (network security groups 957, virtual networks 205, Traffic Manager profiles 294): because networking and traffic management are already commonly pre-provisioned, pre-create and validate target VNets, NSGs, public IPs and DNS/Traffic Manager records in the DR region and include their reconfiguration and validation in runbooks to avoid network-level delays during failover.

Recovery Services vaults frequently use user-assigned identities (50% of vaults) and public network access is mostly enabled (98.9%): given this mix of identity and network settings, plan to assign a UAI to ASR vaults for runbook automation, and evaluate/implement vault network controls (private endpoints or service endpoints) as required by compliance while ensuring runbooks can still reach vault endpoints for failover.

Monitoring and alerting artifacts are already in place (datacollectionrules 57, metricalerts 665, scheduled query rules 26, activity log alerts 13): because an alerting/telemetry framework exists, integrate runbook execution events and ASR replication/health metrics into the existing metricalerts and scheduled query rules to produce automated compliance-ready audit trails and escalation workflows.
